revision.text:
'''朝顔形埴輪'''（あさがおがたはにわ）は、器台の上に壺を載せた形状をしており、上部は口縁部が大きく朝顔の花が開いたようにラッパ状に広がっている。
== 概要 == 大きさは、高さ50～110センチメートルぐらい。円筒部にタガ状の突帯が3～7本あることや透かし孔があることなどが[[円筒埴輪]]と同じ点である。 この[[埴輪]]は、[[3世紀]]の[[古墳]]にはなく、[[4世紀]]の古墳から現れる。[[6世紀]]の埴輪の歴史が終わるまで存続する。 古い例では、壺の胴部上半が円筒からはみ出した形であるが、新しい例は壺の頸・胴部が省略され、口縁部が円筒に直接つながる形になる。
[[奈良県]]布留遺跡からの出土品が有名であるが、円筒埴輪をもつほとんどの古墳から出土しており、東は[[山形県]]・[[岩手県]]から西は[[鹿児島県]]まで広く分布している。 [[三重県]]石山古墳では円筒埴輪4、5本につき1本の割合で本埴輪が配置されている。 <!-- == 脚注 == <references /> --> == 参考文献 == *春成秀爾「朝顔形埴輪」/田中琢・佐原真編『日本考古学事典』三省堂 2003年 ISBN 978-4-385-15835-8
== 関連項目 == *[[埴輪]] *[[形象埴輪]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:なかやまおおつかこふん}} {{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:古墳時代]]

revision.text:
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ({{ill|Yanai|ja|柳井市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher={{ill|Sanseido|ja|三省堂}}|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} * {{ill|Special pedestal and pottery|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}} * {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}} * {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪}}

These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher={{ill|Sanseido|ja|三省堂}}|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}

At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

revision.text:
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher={{ill|Sanseido|ja|三省堂}}|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} * {{ill|Special pedestal and pottery|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} * {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa}}

[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi burial mounds|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century burial mounds and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most burial mounds with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Morning glory-shaped haniwa}}[[Category:11]] <!--Morning glory-shaped haniwa--> [[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]][[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]][[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]][[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]][[sn:Morning glory-shaped haniwa]][[Category:11]] [[ja:朝顔形埴輪]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher={{ill|Sanseido|ja|三省堂}}|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}
== Related articles == * {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} * {{ill|Special pedestal and pottery|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} * {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}} * {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Figural Haniwa}}

These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|柳井茶臼山古墳}} ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}})]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankokan Museum|ja|天理大学附属天理参考館}})]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|ja|保渡田古墳群}} ({{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|ja|群馬県}})]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|lt=Special platforms and jars}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|lt=Kibi region|ja|吉備|en}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡}} in {{ill|Tenri|ja|天理市}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|ja|山形県}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|ja|鹿児島県}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Morning glory–shaped haniwa]][[ja:朝顔形埴輪]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11517786]]
Wikidata name: morning glory-shaped haniwa
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at [[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]])]]
[[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} ([[:en:Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|Tenri University Sankokan Museum]])]]
[[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]])]]

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Sources ==
* {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Archaeological Research Society|qq=|1=Kōkogaku Kenkyūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610682}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}}
* {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher=[[:en:Sanseidō|Sanseido]]|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaka City Museum|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}

* {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figurative Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}

They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

Wikidata name: morning glory-shaped haniwa
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at [[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]])]]
[[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} ([[:en:Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|Tenri University Sankokan Museum]])]]
[[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]])]]

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Morning glory–shaped haniwa]][[ja:朝顔形埴輪]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11517786]]
Wikidata name: morning glory-shaped haniwa
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at [[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]])]]
[[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} ([[:en:Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|Tenri University Sankokan Museum]])]]
[[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]])]]

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Sources ==
* {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Archaeological Research Society|1=Kōkogaku Kenkyūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610682}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}}
* {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher=[[:en:Sanseidō|Sanseido]]|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaka City Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}

* {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figurative Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}

[[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]])]]

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Morning glory–shaped haniwa]][[ja:朝顔形埴輪]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Morning glory–shaped haniwa]][[ja:朝顔形埴輪]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Morning glory–shaped haniwa]][[ja:朝顔形埴輪]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11517786]] Wikidata name: morning glory-shaped haniwa [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at [[:en:Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun]] ([[:en:Yanai,_Yamaguchi|Yanai]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]])]] [[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]], excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} ([[:en:Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|Tenri University Sankokan Museum]])]] [[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Cluster]] ([[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma Prefecture]])]]
'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.
== Overview ==
The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>
Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.
While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * {{Cite journal|和書|last=Kondo|first=Yoshiro|authorlink=近藤義郎|last2=Harunari|first2=Shuji|authorlink2=春成秀爾|year=1967|date=February 1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|issue=3|pages=13-35|publisher={{ill|Archaeological Research Society|ja|考古学研究会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Archaeological Research Society|1=Kōkogaku Kenkyūkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11610682}}|issn=03869148|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|last=Harunari|first=Shuji|author1=Tanaka, Taku|authorlink1=田中琢|author2=Sahara, Makoto|authorlink2=佐原真|chapter=Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa|title=Japanese Archaeological Dictionary|publisher=[[:en:Sanseidō|Sanseido]]|year=2003|isbn=9784385158358|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author={{ill|Asaka City Museum|ja|朝霞市博物館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asaka City Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|title=The World of Haniwa (40th Anniversary Special Exhibition)|publisher=Asaka City|year=2007|ncid=BA83022751|ref=harv}}
== Related articles == * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * {{ill|Special pedestal and pottery|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Special pedestal and pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} * {{ill|Cylindrical Haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}} * {{ill|Figurative Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figurative Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] (2nd century), particularly in the [[:en:Kibi|Kibi region]] (present-day [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from [[:en:Yamagata Prefecture|Yamagata Prefecture]] and [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]] in the east to [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|WD=Q404065|lt=Kibi region}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Yanai Chausu-yama Kofun 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa lined up at {{ill|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|en|Yanai Chausuyama Kofun|WD=Q11534089|lt=Yanai Chausuyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Yanai,_Yamaguchi|en|Yanai,_Yamaguchi|WD=Q819678|lt=Yanai}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|WD=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture}})]]
[[File:布留遺跡出土 埴輪・土器.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped haniwa along with cylindrical haniwa and {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}}, excavated from {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} ({{ill|Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|en|Tenri University Sankōkan Museum|WD=Q17229261|lt=Tenri University Sankokan Museum}})]]
[[File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|thumb|200px|Morning glory-shaped and cylindrical haniwa at Hachimanzuka Kofun in the {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}})]]

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|WD=Q404065|lt=Kibi region}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|WD=Q404065|lt=Kibi region}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|WD=Q404065|lt=Kibi region|1=Kibi}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|WD=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|WD=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.

'''Morning Glory-Shaped Haniwa''' (朝顔形埴輪, Asagao-gata Haniwa) are a type of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. They are classified under the broader Category of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cylindrical haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Cylindrical Haniwa|qq=}}. These haniwa are characterized by a shape resembling a stand with a vase on top, with an upper section flaring outwards like the trumpet-shaped bloom of a morning glory flower.

== Overview ==

The haniwa of the Kofun period are thought to trace their origins to the {{ill|specialized stands and jars|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/specialized stands and jars|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} used in ritual offerings at {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}} in the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} (2nd century), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|qid=Q404065|lt=Kibi region|1=Kibi}} (present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}).<ref>{{harvnb|Kondo|Harunari|1967|pp=13-35}}</ref> Over time, these specialized stands evolved into cylindrical haniwa, while the jars transformed into jar-shaped haniwa. Morning glory-shaped haniwa are believed to have emerged when the stand and jar were combined into a single unit.<ref>{{harvnb|Asaka City Museum|2007|p=3}}</ref>

Typically measuring 50–110 cm in height, these haniwa share features with cylindrical haniwa, such as bands resembling hoops (3–7 in number) and openwork designs. Morning glory-shaped haniwa first appeared in 4th-century Kofuns and persisted throughout the haniwa tradition, which ended in the 6th century. Early examples show the jar's body partially protruding from the cylindrical base, but over time, the jar's neck and body were omitted, and the flared mouth connected directly to the cylinder.

While notable examples have been excavated from the {{ill|Furu Ruins|ja|布留遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Furu Ruins|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, similar haniwa are found in most Kofuns with cylindrical haniwa, ranging from {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} and {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}} in the east to {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} in the west. At the {{ill|Ishiyama Kofun|ja|石山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}, one morning glory-shaped haniwa was placed for every 4–5 cylindrical haniwa.
